text:
A reminder of what the US waiver revocation on Iranian oil sales means -- and doesn't mean: 

Iran can still sell its oil if it finds a willing buyers (and China has been in the past, and will be again). Yes, Iran will need to discount its oil, but that would be mostly offset by the rise in oil prices triggered by the fresh hostilities. The biggest problem for Iran? It would have to use (again) non-US dollar settlement.

Put it all together, and Tehran would be able to sell the ~60 million barrels it has put on the water so far since the blockade was lifted, at relatively good prices (~$65-70 a barrel), making probably ~$4 billion. 

Only a return to the US Navy blockade would stop Iran from selling its oil. But that means a new war.

According to Reuters, China has lifted refined fuel export restrictions for ​the rest of July and allowed a private refiner to resume shipments after a 4-month halt. 
https://t.co/xF2hvSAlgw

US President Donald Trump on Iran:

Ceasefire is over "as far as I’m concerned."

"I don’t want to deal with them anymore," he says. However, then he says he would let his negotiators keep talking. But "I think they’re wasting their time."
President Trump says US probably will strike Iran for a second time tonight.

The IMF has left its forecast for economic activity almost unchanged from what it predicted back in April. It forecast now global GDP growth this year at 3.0% (from 3.1% in Apr) and 3.4% for 2027 (from 3.2% in Apr). Put is simply, the war has not triggered an economic crisis.
quoted_text: Our latest World Economic Outlook Update projects growth at 3.0% in 2026 and 3.4% in 2027, broadly unchanged from April. Two forces are shaping the outlook: the negative supply shock from the Middle East war and positive technology momentum driven by AI. https://t.co/EWThm8NLlj https://t.co/HUmrvRtymd

text: We are back to the days of Donald "the-madman" Trump threatening to take over Kharg Island (never happened then; won't happen now), blow up Iran's bridges, and even its water desalination plants (the later most likely a war crime under international law).
BREAKING: Russia bans diesel exports, further tightening the global supply and demand balance for refined products.

@AlessandroPonz4 I had been buying the dip, and thus strengthened my strategic olive oil reserve. Mostly Spanish origin, but I took the unprecedented step recently of accepting some Greek and Italian origin delivery (I blame @cbjom for it).

@IndiScipio “… for refined products.”

(All what it takes is reading)

CHART OF THE DAY: This is how the IMF believes the oil market has adjusted to the Strait of Hormuz disruption. 

“The global economy as a whole has, so far, weathered the shock from the war better than feared,” the IMF said in an update of its World Economic Outlook report. https://t.co/ZuRUyaz9Pt

Having a bit of déjà vu:

President Trump says expects Iran flare-up to be over “very quickly.”

(The appetite in the White House — and Tehran, for that matter — for a new war seems very limited, despite what hardliners on both sides indicate)

Iran and Qatari top diplomats held a phone call to discuss what Tehran described as “recent events in the Strait of Hormuz” 

(“events” is quite the word to describe what happened: Iranian drones hit a laden Qatari LNG tanker in Omani territorial waters)
